title: Retail Derivatives and Sentiment: A Sentiment Measure Constructed from Issuances of Retail Structured Equity Products
abstract: We use retail structured equity product (SEP) issuances to construct a new sentiment measure for large capitalization stocks. The SEP sentiment measure predicts negative abnormal returns on the SEP reference stocks based on a variety of factor models, and also predicts returns in Fama-MacBeth regressions that include a wide range of covariates. Consistent with our interpretation that SEP issuances reflect investor sentiment, aggregate SEP issuances are highly correlated with the Baker-Wurgler sentiment index. Tobit regressions reveal that proxies for attention and sentiment predict SEP issuance volumes, providing additional evidence consistent with the hypothesis that SEP issuances reflect sentiment.

article.introduction:
We propose a new measureof retail investor sentiment for individual stocks that is constructed from issuance volumes of retail structured equity products (SEPs) that provide synthetic long exposure to the SEPs’ reference stocks. Stocks for which SEP issuance volumes are high during a month experience large negative abnormal returns during the next month. The negative abnormal returns persist robustly across a wide range of benchmarks, including standard and contemporary factor models.1Weighted by proceeds, 81% of SEPs reference stocks with market capitalizations falling in the top decile of Center for Research on Securities Prices (CRSP) stocks, and 89% of SEPs are based on top-quintile stocks. Thus, SEPs issuance volumes predict negative abnormal returns on widely followed, liquid, large capitalization stocks that are generally easy to borrow and sell short.
  Our interpretation of this predictability is that SEPs issuance volumes proxy for retail investor sentiment, defined as beliefs about future stock prices that are not justified by rational evaluation of available information.2We hypothesize that high issuance volumes are correlated with high retail investor sentiment and overvaluation, which is subsequently corrected. This interpretation is based on two features of the SEPs market. First, the pricing of SEPs based on individual stocks is extremely disadvantageous to retail investors. Henderson and Pearson (2011) find that the offering prices of one popular brand of SEPs, Morgan Stanley Stock Participating Accreting Redemption Quarterly-Pay Securities (SPARQS), were on average about 8% greater than reasonable estimates of the SPARQS's fair values. The SPARQS had an original time to maturity of about one year, so that if the underlying stock was fairly priced, the 8% markup implies that a SPARQS investor was accepting an abnormal return or “alpha” of about −8% per year. Vokatá (2021) studies a more recent and much larger sample comprising almost the universe of U.S. yield enhancement products (YEPs). She finds average mispricing of similar magnitude, and that the payoffs of a large fraction of the products are dominated by simple combinations of the underlying asset and listed options. Given the overpricing and inferiority to portfolios of listed options, it is unlikely that many sophisticated investors purchase SEPs.3
  Second, the SEPs marketing process reveals information about the demands of the unsophisticated retail investors who purchase SEPs. As described in Egan (2019), issuers offer products based on a range of underlying stocks, often on a monthly cycle, and then investors make their purchase decisions from among the offered SEPs. Given this model of SEPs distribution, issuers presumably design their offering menus to include the stocks they expect retail investors to demand, and then, of course, the investors’ purchase decisions reveal their preferences from among the underlying stocks offered. Through this process, SEP issuances reveal the stocks that interest unsophisticated investors.
  SEPs issuance volumes are typically small relative to the market capitalizations and trading volumes of their underlying stocks. We hypothesize that the SEPs sentiment measure is correlated with overvaluation and predicts stock returns because the demands of the investors who purchase SEPs are correlated with the demands of those who purchase the underlying stocks rather than the SEPs.
  In addition to the main abnormal return results, we provide a range of other evidence consistent with the hypothesis that SEP issues reflect sentiment. This includes showing that an aggregate market-wide measure of SEP issuance volumes is highly correlated with the well-known Baker and Wurgler (2006) sentiment index, and that proxies for investor attention and sentiment predict SEP issuances. We also find that a significant part of the negative abnormal return is realized during short windows around earnings announcement dates, consistent with overvaluation being corrected as information is released.
  We construct the sentiment measure using data on SEPs based on a single common stock that have positive exposure to the underlying as of the pricing date, and are issued during the period running from January 2004 through December 2019.4We construct the measure for each month and underlying reference stock as the ratio of SEPs issuance proceeds to the underlying stock's market capitalization, normalized by the sum of the ratios. Due to the normalization, the sentiment measures sum to one and thus are interpretable as portfolio weights. We then test whether this measure predicts future stock returns.
  Specifically, we use the portfolio weights to construct portfolios of the underlying reference stocks, and then examine the returns of the portfolios. We find that the SEPs-based sentiment measure predicts large negative abnormal returns on the underlying stocks during the month following the portfolio formation month. Using the Fama and French (1993) three-, Carhart (1997) four-, and Fama and French (2015) five-factor models as benchmarks, the abnormal returns based on these models are −1.25% (t-statistic −4.51), −1.23% (t-statistic −4.45), and −1.09% (t-statistic −3.83), respectively. These results are consistent with the hypothesis that retail demand revealed by SEPs issuances is an indicator of sentiment that causes some stocks to be overvalued, resulting in negative returns as the overvaluation is reversed.
  We also consider alternative benchmarks consisting of the four- and five-factor production-based orq-factor models described by Hou, Xue, and Zhang (2015) and Hou et al. (2021), the Stambaugh and Yuan (2017) mispricing four-factor model, and the Daniel, Hirshleifer, and Sun (2020) behaviorally motivated three-factor model. Using these models, the alphas during the first month after portfolio formation are −1.11% (t-statistic −4.06), −0.93% (t-statistic −3.30), −1.14% (t-statistic −3.69), and −0.92% (t-statistic −3.41), respectively. Thus, the predictability is slightly mitigated using behaviorally motivated factors compared to using the other factors. This suggests that the SEP sentiment measure shares a common component with the behavioral factors, which is to be expected. However, most of the abnormal return survives benchmarking using the mispricing and behaviorally motivated factors. The SEP-sentiment-weighted portfolio returns have significantly negative loadings on the Hou et al. (2021) expected growth factorEG, the Stambaugh and Yuan (2017) management factorMGMT, and the Daniel, Hirshleifer, and Sun (2020) financing and post-earning-announcement-drift factorsFINandPEAD.
  We also construct and add to all of the models, additional factors based on variables that have been shown to predict stock returns. These consist of factors based on idiosyncratic volatility (IVOL (Ang et al. (2006)), short interest) (Akbas et al. (2017)), the 52-week high (George and Hwang (2004)), short-term reversals, and lottery characteristics (Kumar (2009), Bali, Cakici, and Whitelaw (2011)). After controlling for these factors, the alphas during the first month after portfolio formation are similar to those obtained when the additional factors are not included. An alternative approach using Fama and MacBeth (1973) cross-sectional regressions also finds that SEP sentiment predicts negative stock returns controlling for other variables.
  We validate our interpretation of SEP issues as reflecting investor sentiment by providing additional evidence consistent with this hypothesis. Our SEP sentiment measure is for individual stocks and thus is not directly comparable to the well-known Baker-Wurgler sentiment index, which is a market-wide measure. However, we use the SEP data to construct an aggregate market-wide measure and compare that to the Baker-Wurgler index. The aggregate measure constructed from SEP issuance volumes is highly correlated with the Baker-Wurgler index.
  Panel Tobit regressions show that SEP issuance volumes are predicted by proxies for investor attention and sentiment, including Google search volumes, the Stambaugh, Yu, and Yuan (2015) mispricing measure, the Baker-Wurgler sentiment index, past returns, and past realized stock return volatilities. This is also consistent with SEP issues reflecting a combination of investor sentiment and attention.
  We also find that a significant fraction of the abnormal return subsequent to SEP issues is realized during a short window around the first earnings announcement following the SEP issue. This is consistent with biased expectations being partially corrected when information is released. Cross-sectional regressions show that the SEP sentiment measure helps explain underlying stock returns over short windows around earnings announcement dates.
  Our paper is related to the literature on retail investors and stock prices. Early literature provides evidence that retail demand can drive stock prices away from fundamental values, consistent with retail demand being a proxy for investor sentiment and with the view of retail investors as noise traders (Hvidkjaer (2008), Barber, Odean, and Zhu (2009)). However, more recent studies conclude that retail traders profit by providing liquidity to other investors and that retail order flow predicts future stock returns (Kelley and Tetlock (2013), Chen et al. (2014), Kelley and Tetlock (2017), Barrot, Kaniel, and Sraer (2016), Boehmer et al. (2021)).5Using sentiment proxies based on mutual fund flows, Frazzini and Lamont (2008) construct a proxy for retail demand for different stocks from quarterly mutual fund flows and find that the measure predicts short-term positive and long-run negative future stock returns. On the other hand, Keswani and Stolin (2008) use monthly mutual fund flow data and find robust evidence for a smart money effect in buying (but not selling) decisions made by both individuals and institutions.
  Our SEPs sentiment measure differs from these other measures because it is based on the purchases of the subset of retail investors who buy SEPs and thus are almost certainly unsophisticated. In contrast to the measures based on mutual fund flows, the selection of stocks is not made by mutual fund managers. Our measure is also available on a monthly, rather than a quarterly, basis, allowing us to study the relation between retail investor demand and subsequent returns over a monthly horizon.
  The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIdescribes the data we use, focusing on the SEPs issuance data. SectionIIdetails our construction of the sentiment measure from the SEPs issuance volumes. SectionIIIexamines the relation between this sentiment measure and future stock returns. SectionIVprovides additional evidence consistent with the hypothesis that SEP issues reflect sentiment. SectionVbriefly concludes.
article.I. U.S. Retail SEPs:
SEPs are equity-linked notes typically issued by financial institutions, that is, investment banks or investment banking subsidiaries of commercial banks. They are liabilities of the issuer but have payoffs linked to the stock price of an unrelated company, a stock index, or a basket of stocks or stock indexes. We use only SEPs based on individual equities because our goal is to construct a sentiment measure for individual stocks.
  SEP issuers file shelf registration statements and pricing supplements to the registration statements or free writing prospectuses that describe each offering.6We collect the filings and extract the SEPs’ terms. In this study, we use the reference asset (i.e., the underlying stock), the issue proceeds (size), and the pricing or trade date; we use the other terms only to exclude some issues (e.g., those with inverse exposure to the underlying stock price) from the sample. We match the SEPs reference stocks to the CRSP database using the name and/or ticker symbol of the underlying stock extracted from the filings.
  The sample consists of SEPs that offered synthetic long exposure to a single common stock as of the pricing or trade date and were issued during the period running from January 2004 through December 2019. Four of the SEPs issued during our sample period are “inverse” or “bearish” products that did not provide long exposure to the reference stocks, and we do not include these products in the sample. After dropping these products, the sample consists of a total of 39,510 SEP issues.
  A total of 1,036 of the sample SEPs are “dual-directional” or “absolute return” products in which the maturity date payoff is a decreasing function of the stock price for a range of stock prices and an increasing function of the stock price for other ranges of stock prices. These 1,036 SEPs have nine different structures. We implement valuation models for one example of each of the nine structures and find that their values are increasing functions of the prices of the reference stocks on the pricing or trade date. This analysis is described in theInternet Appendix.7We include the dual-directional and absolute return products in the sample because, as this analysis shows, they provide long exposure to the reference stock on the trade date. TheInternet Appendixalso reports the results of repeating the main analyses after dropping these 1,036 dual-directional and absolute return issues from the sample. The results when we drop these issues are almost identical to the results when we retain these issues in the sample.
  Figure1presents the monthly number of sample SEP issues (dotted blue line, left scale), the number of unique stocks underlying the SEP issues (dashed red line, left scale), and the aggregate principal amounts of SEPs issues (solid black line, right scale) during the period running from January 2000 through December 2019. Prior to 2004, SEPs monthly issuance activity was low: the number of issues per month never exceeded 20 and the total principal amount was always below $200 million. There was a reasonable cross section of underlying stocks starting from early 2006, when there were SEPs issues on 24 and 36 reference stocks during January and February, respectively. After 2006, monthly SEPs issuances and proceeds continued to increase, peaking at 431 issues during March 2017 and $1.5 billion of proceeds during January 2008. During the 2008 financial crisis, SEPs issuances contracted, as did equity market capitalizations, but recovered in late 2009.
  Based on the patterns shown in the figure, we think January 2004 is the early plausible sample start date, while January 2006 is probably the best sample start date. We carry out the main analyses using both the 2004 to 2019 and 2006 to 2019 samples, and find that the main results are robust to the choice of the sample start date. Because the cross section is much better populated starting in early 2006, we use the 2006 to 2019 sample for the cross-sectional analyses.
article.A. SEP Structures: The two most common SEP structures are reverse convertible notes and autocallable notes. Each of these structures has two common variants, which we describe next.
article.A.1. Reverse Convertibles:
A reverse convertible can be interpreted as a high coupon bond together with a written put option on the reference stock, where the put may be either vanilla or down-and-in. In either case, the strike price of the put is typically equal to the pricing date closing price of the reference stock. If the put is down-and-in, the barrier or in-strike is frequently 20% to 30% below the initial price of the reference stock. Typical times to maturity range from three months to one year, accounting for 95% of this subsample.
  When the embedded put option is down-and-in, the daily closing price of the reference stock is compared to a barrier level on each date during a monitoring period, which is normally the period from and excluding the pricing date to and including the valuation date. The barrier level, which is given various names, is often about 70% or 80% of the initial stock price. If the stock price never crosses the barrier during the monitoring period, the maturity payoff is the face value of the note. If the stock price crosses the barrier, the maturity payoff is typicallyV(ST,T) = min((F/S0)ST,F), that is, it is equivalent to the payoff of a portfolio consisting of a zero-coupon bond with a face value ofFand written vanilla puts with a strike of S0onF/S0shares of stock.
article.A.2. Autocallable Notes:
The defining feature of an autocallable note is that there is a list of call dates, typically quarterly but sometimes monthly, on which the note is automatically called if the stock price exceeds a specified level, which is typically the initial stock price. About 52% of the sample issues are autocallables, making this the most common structure in our sample. The principal amount of these issues is usually either $10 or $1,000, and the initial times to maturity range from three months to six years, with 60% of the issues having terms between one and three years.
  Often the interest payments are contingent on the reference stock price observed on an observation date shortly before the interest payment date, with interest being paid only if the closing stock price on the corresponding observation date is above a specified threshold level, which is often about 70% or 75% of the initial stock price. A total of 78% of the autocallables in our sample that pay interest have such contingent interest payments.12Other autocallable SEPs pay fixed interest, regardless of the performance of the reference stock.
  In theInternet Appendix, we show that the vast majority of the sample SEPs are YEPs. The coupon or “headline rate” features prominently in the marketing of YEPs, often being included in the product name (Célérier and Vallée (2017), Vokatá (2021)). In such structures, investors take on downside risk in exchange for the high coupon. Why do investors purchase such structures?
  Célérier and Vallée (2017, p. 1502) draw on the theoretical work of Bordalo, Gennaioli, and Shleifer (2012, 2013,2016)) on salience and consumer choice to explain why retail investors purchase YEPs. In that framework, “a lottery payoff is salient if it is very different in percentage terms from the payoffs of other available lotteries (in the same state of the world)” (Bordalo, Gennaioli, and Shleifer (2012, p. 1244)), while Bordalo, Gennaioli, and Shleifer (2013, p. 623) write “An asset's salient payoff is naturally defined as one most different from the average market payoff in a given state of the world.” High headline rates are salient because in many states of the world, for example, when most stock prices decline or remain approximately constant, they are higher than the returns available on other securities. This is especially true during periods of low interest rates. Bordalo, Gennaioli, and Shleifer (2016, pp. 497−499) use the salience framework to explain the phenomenon of “reaching for yield” in the market for mortgage-backed securities. Salience can also lead to demand for securities with positively skewed payoffs, as discussed in Bordalo, Gennaioli, and Shleifer (2013), because the large payoffs in certain states of the world will be salient.
  Célérier and Vallée (2017) provide evidence that this framework explains the demand for YEPs. They argue that issuers design structured products to cater to yield-seeking investors and shroud the downside risk, writing “Retail investors may be prone to salient thinking and consequently may overweight headline rates, and neglect risk,” especially “when making investment decisions in low interest rate environments” (Célérier and Vallée (2017, p. 1502)). Consistent with the implications of the salience framework, in their sample of European SEPs they find that: (i) the issuers offer products with higher headline rates, and rely more on complexity, during periods of low interest rates; (ii) the share of risky products increases over the sample period, when rates were generally decreasing; (iii) product markups are increasing with both headline rates and product complexity; and (iv) the product marketing focuses on the headline rate. The first two findings are consistent with the salience framework, as that theory predicts that investors attend to high headline rates in an environment of decreasing interest rates, while the third is consistent with investors being more likely to neglect downside risk and the issuer having more scope to capture hidden markups in complex products offering higher headline rates. The alternative hypothesis, that the products are designed to enable better risk sharing, does not have these implications.14Vokatá (2021, p. 740) also concludes that her results for U.S. retail YEPs “are consistent with behavioral explanations, such as framing and salience (Bordalo, Gennaioli, and Shleifer (2016)), playing an important role in reaching for yield by retail investors.”
  When noise traders are optimistic about particular securities, it pays [for] arbitrageurs to create more of them. These securities might be mutual funds, new share issues, penny oil stocks, or junk bonds: anything that is overpriced at the moment. It also pays to carve up corporate cash flows in ways that make the securities with claims to these flows most attractive to investors. … Just as entrepreneurs spend resources to build casinos to take advantage of gamblers, arbitrageurs build investment banks and brokerage firms to predict and feed noise trader demand.
  Thus, we expect SEP issuances to reveal the stocks demanded by unsophisticated retail investors.
article.C. Characteristics of the SEP Reference:
The majority of the SEPs’ underlying stocks have large market capitalizations. For example, 82% (70%) of the issues are based on underlying stocks that fall into the largest market capitalization quintile (decile) as of the last trading day of the month prior to the issue month. Approximately, 89% (81%) of the total proceeds are due to issues based on top market-capitalization quintile (decile) stocks. Such stocks are typically easy to borrow and sell short.
  We categorize the sample SEPs based on the industry of the reference stock using the Fama-French 12-industry classification.15Figure2presents the time series of the total proceeds of issues based on stocks in the three most frequent reference industries, which are Business Equipment, Energy, and Finance. Stocks of companies in the Business Equipment industry are the most frequent reference stocks. The only times when Business Equipment was not the dominant industry for SEPs reference equities were during the recovery following the 2008 crisis and April 2018, when SEPs frequently referenced stocks in the Finance industry.
article.D. Additional Data: Our analyses use additional data. Stock prices and returns are from the databases maintained by the CRSP. Standard industry classification (SIC) codes come from Compustat, and from CRSP when the SIC codes are not available in the Compustat database. We obtain factor portfolio returns for the Fama-French (1993, 2015)) factors, the Carhart (1997) momentum factor, the short-term reversal factor, and one of the two IVOL factors from Kenneth French's data library.16The returns on the Hou, Xue, and Zhang (2015) and Hou et al. (2021)qfactors are from Lu Zhang's data library.17The returns on the Daniel, Hirshleifer, and Sun (2020) behavioral factors are from Lin Sun's website,18and the Stambaugh, Yu, and Yuan (2015) mispricing proxies and Stambaugh and Yuan (2017) mispricing factor returns are from Robert Stambaugh's website.19We construct the returns on the second IVOL factor, the 52-week high factor, and the lottery factor (LOT) from the CRSP data. We obtain Google trends search volume directly from Google's website.20The Baker-Wurgler sentiment indexSENTis from Jeffrey Wurgler's website.21Option trading volumes and implied volatilities are from Option Metrics. Earnings announcement dates come from IBES.
article.II. Construction of the SEP Sentiment Me:
Our SEP sentiment measure is computed from issuances, and thus investor purchases, of newly issued SEPs that offer long exposure to a single common stock as of the pricing or trade date. To compute the sentiment measure for stocknduring montht, we first compute the total proceeds (dollar amount)Qt,n${Q_{t,n}}$of all SEP issues linked to stocknin montht. When a stock is not referenced by any SEP issued during montht, thenQt,n${Q_{t,n}}$= 0.
  Due to the normalization, the sentiment measuresGt,nsum to one and thus can be used as portfolio weights.
  For example, in June 2015 there were 26 SEP issues linked to Ford Motor common stock with total proceeds of $88.49 million. During the same month, there were 253 single-stock SEPs on 113 unique stocks with total proceeds of $644.76 million. Using Ford Motor's end of May market capitalization of $59.23 billion, the proceeds of $88.49 million representedQt,n/Vt−1,n= 0.15% of its end of May market capitalization. The sentiment measure for Ford Motor is then this ratio, normalized by the sum of the same ratios for all stocks so that the sentiment measures sum to one. Despite the fact that the proceeds of SEPs based on Ford Motor accounted for 13.72% ($88.49 / $644.76) of total SEP proceeds, Ford Motor's sentiment measure for June 2015 was onlyGt,n= 3.60% due to its large equity market capitalization.
  Figure3presents the time series of the main SEP sentiment measureGt,nfor several stocks to illustrate the variation of this measure over time. Panel A shows the sentiment measures for the five stocks having the largest average sentiment values among the stocks having at least 60 months of data during the sample period. Those five stocks are: Chesapeake Energy, Delta Air Lines, Under Armour, United Rentals, and U.S. Steel. Panel B shows the time series ofGt,nfor the five stocks (Caterpillar, Dow Chemical, General Motors, Micron Tech, and Schlumberger) that have sentiment values closest to the median value among stocks having at least 60 months of data during the sample period. Both figures show that there is considerable time-series variation in the measureGt,n, indicating that the measure does not simply capture a stock fixed effect.
article.III. SEP Sentiment and Return Predictabi:
We hypothesize that retail demand for SEPs issuances serves as an indicator of investor sentiment that causes some stocks to be overvalued. This hypothesis implies that the stocks underlying SEPs will underperform risk-adjusted benchmarks following SEPs issuance, consistent with “noise trader” theory (DeLong et al. (1990, 1991)), Shleifer and Summers (1990)).
  We use calendar-time portfolio return regressions to examine the ability of the SEP sentiment measure to predict future stock returns. The portfolio stocks are those referenced by SEPs issued during the previous month. If SEPs reveal sentiment associated with overpricing, then the portfolio of SEPs reference stocks will underperform risk-adjusted benchmarks. Thus, we test the null hypothesis that the average subsequent abnormal returns to the portfolios, that is, the regression intercepts, are equal to zero.
  This procedure results in monthly calendar-time portfolio returns in which stocks are weighted by their SEPs sentiment measure. We then compute excess returnsRt+1–rf,t, whererf,tis the one-month risk-free rate from the end of montht, and test the predictive ability of the SEPs sentiment measure by estimating time-series regressions using standard factor models: the Fama and French (1993) three-, Carhart (1997) four-, and Fama and French (2015) five-factor models. If the SEPs sentiment measure predicts returns, then we expect the estimate of the abnormal return, or regression intercept, to be negative. The null hypothesis is that the regression intercepts or abnormal returns of the calendar-time portfolio returns are zero.
  TableIreports the coefficient estimates and correspondingt-statistics for the various factor models. The results in the left-hand part of the table use the SEPs sentiment measure beginning in 2004, while the right-hand columns present results for the sample period starting in 2006, at which point the SEPs market had further matured. Across all six regressions, the intercepts or alphas are always negative, ranging from −1.33% (t-statistic −4.57) to −1.09% (t-statistic −3.83). The negative and statistically significant intercepts show that the portfolios of SEPs reference stocks, weighted by their relative popularity among SEPs investors, underperform the standard risk-adjusted benchmarks. The magnitudes of the intercepts are economically meaningful. Thus, the SEPs sentiment measure predicts economically and statistically significant negative abnormal returns after accounting for standard risk factors. These negative abnormal returns are consistent with subsequent corrections to overpricing of the reference stocks during the months of high SEPs issuance.
  To check whether our results are driven by the returns in only part of the sample period, we plot the time series of the realized abnormal returns (that is, the sum of the intercept and residual) computed using the Fama-French three-factor model. Figure4presents two plots, the realized abnormal returns (solid line), and the 12-month moving average of the realized abnormal returns (dashed line). Both plots show seemingly random variation over time and indicate that the ability of our SEP-based sentiment measure to predict returns is not concentrated in any period. We also compute the Durbin-Watson test statistic for first-order autocorrelation of the realized abnormal returns and find that it is 1.84, close to 2. This confirms the visual impression of no important positive serial correlation in the realized abnormal returns.
  Several recent asset pricing models propose alternative or additional factors, some of which are behaviorally motivated. We estimate the abnormal returns of the calendar-time portfolios relative to these benchmarks. The alternative models we use are the production-basedq-factor model of Hou, Xue, and Zhang (2015), theq5-factor model of Hou et al. (2021), the Stambaugh and Yuan (2017) mispricing factor model, and the Daniel, Hirshleifer, and Sun (2020) behavioral factor model. Benchmarking the SEPs sentiment portfolio returns using these factor models allows us to ascertain whether the negative abnormal returns evident using traditional factor models are explained by the investment factors or any of the behavioral or mispricing factors.
  Panels A to D of TableIIreport the results of the calendar-time regressions of the returns on the portfolio of SEPs reference stocks benchmarked using the four alternative models. Scanning the panels, the estimated intercepts (alphas) are all negative and statistically significant, but smaller than the estimates for the traditional factor models reported in TableI. Specifically, the estimates of alpha range from −0.92% (t-statistic −3.41) in the Hou et al. (2021)q5-factor model to −1.17% (t-statistic −3.59) for the Stambaugh and Yuan (2017) model estimated using the sample starting in 2006, respectively. The results, that the abnormal returns are smaller using the Daniel, Hirshleifer, and Sun (2020) behavioral factors as compared to the traditional Fama-French factors and the Hou, Xue, and Zhang (2015) production-based factors, are consistent with the SEPs sentiment measure sharing a common component with the behavioral factors. However, SEPs’ ability to predict future stock returns persists after controlling for these additional factors, consistent with the hypothesis that SEPs issuances provide incremental information about sentiment over and beyond the existing behavioral and mispricing factors.
  Similar to the results for the traditional factor models, the coefficients on the market factor are large and highly significant in all specifications. The results presented in Panel B show that the SEPs sentiment-weighted portfolios’ returns have a large and significant negative loading on the Hou et al. (2021) expected growth factorEG. This negative relation indicates that the SEPs stocks tend to have low expected growth.
  The results for the Stambaugh and Yuan (2017) model reveal that the SEP sentiment-weighted portfolio returns have large negative and highly significant loadings on the management-related factorMGMT. The coefficient estimates andt-statistics reported in Panels A to C show that the SEPs sentiment portfolio does not have significant loadings on the performance-related factorsPERFandROE. Referring to TableII, Panel D, the SEPs sentiment-weighted portfolios’ returns have large and statistically significant negative loadings on the Daniel, Hirshleifer, and Sun (2020) short- and long-horizon behavioral factorsPEADandFIN. These negative coefficients indicate that the SEPs stocks tend to be ones with high long-run financing activity, but low earnings surprises in the short run.
  We also form equal-weighted portfolios of SEP stocks in each month and repeat the analyses for which we report results in TablesIandII. We report the results of these additional analyses inInternet AppendixTableIAV. The alphas of the equally weighted portfolios are negative, but considerably smaller than those of the portfolios formed weighting by SEP issuance volumes. They are between −53 bps (t-statistic −3.37) for the Fama and French (1993) three-factor model and −24 bps (t-statistic −1.61) for the Hou et al. (2021)q5-factor model. This finding, that the abnormal returns are smaller for equally weighted returns, indicates that the SEP issuance volume, and not just the fact of a SEP issue, is useful in predicting returns.
  Other variables known to predict stock returns include IVOL (Ang et al. (2006)), short interest (Akbas et al. (2017)), the 52-week high (George and Hwang (2004)), short-term reversals (Jegadeesh (1990)), and lottery characteristics (Kumar (2009), Bali, Cakici, and Whitelaw (2011)). We add factors based on each of these variables to the various regression models for which we report results in TablesIandIIand examine whether the inclusion of the additional factors impacts the findings discussed above.
  IVOL has been shown to predict the cross section of stock returns (Ang et al. (2006)), with high-IVOL stocks experiencing negative returns in the next month.22We use two different IVOL factors, both of which involve value-weighted long positions in high IVOL stocks and value-weighted short positions in low IVOL stocks. The first IVOL factor comes from Kenneth French's data library.23Stocks are sorted into quintile portfolios by their residual variances computed from the daily Fama and French (1993) three-factor model residuals measured over the previous 60 days. The IVOL factor is then the return on a value-weighted portfolio of the 20% of stocks with the highest residual variances minus the return on a value-weighted portfolio of the 20% of stocks with the lowest residual variances. The construction of the second IVOL factor follows Stambaugh, Yu, and Yuan (2015), who compute IVOL using the three-factor model residuals from the preceding month. The IVOL factor is again the difference between the value-weighted returns of the stocks in the top and bottom IVOL quintiles.
  We separately add the IVOL factors to all of the models for which results are reported in TablesIandII. We also estimate models that consist of just the market factor and one of the IVOL factors. The first two sets of columns in TableIIIreport the estimates of the intercepts (abnormal returns) and coefficients on the IVOL factors for the various specifications. For brevity, the coefficient estimates on the other factors are not reported in the table.24The estimated abnormal returns do not change much when we add the IVOL factors to the models: they range from −86 to −117 bps and remain highly statistically significant. In the models formed by adding the IVOL factors to the single-factor market model and the Fama and French (1993(2015)) and Carhart (1997) models, the estimated loadings on the IVOL factors are not large, ranging from 0.2674 (t-statistic 3.37) to 0.3429 (t-statistic 3.57). In the models formed by adding the IVOL factors to the other models, the coefficients on the IVOL factors are small and either not statistically significant or weakly significant.
  Short interest has also been shown to predict negative abnormal returns (Akbas et al. (2017)). Following the literature, each month we sort CRSP common stocks into short interest deciles based onSIt−1$S{I_{t - 1}}$, the ratio of short interest to shares outstanding. Then, we define theSIfactor as value-weighted average returns on a strategy that is long (short) the stocks in the top (bottom) short interest decile. The third set of columns in TableIIIreport the regression results after controlling for this additional factor. The alphas range from −86 to −124 bps, and are statistically significant.
  Another factor of interest is based on the finding that stocks with a large ratio of current price to the highest price achieved within the past 52 weeks, the 52-week high, experience positive returns during the next six months. George and Hwang (2004) interpret this as a form of momentum. We follow the literature and first sort CRSP stocks in each month based on the ratio of current price to the highest price over the past 52 weeks,H52. Then we construct theH52 factor as the value-weighted average return on the strategy of buying the stocks with ratios of current price to the 52-week high falling in the top 30% and shorting the stocks with ratios in the bottom 30%. The fourth set of columns in TableIIIreport the results of models that include this factor. Similar to the other results, we continue to obtain negative and statistically significant estimates of alpha when this additional factor is included in the regression specifications.
  The literature has documented that there are significant short-term reversals in stock returns (e.g., Jegadeesh (1990)). We address this possibility by including the monthly short-term reversal factor (ST_REV) from the French data library in the calendar-time regressions.25The fifth set of columns in TableIIIshows the results. The alphas are still negative, between −93 and −134 bps, and statistically significant.
  Finally, we examine whether including a factor based on stocks’ lottery characteristics impacts the results. It has been documented that there is a significant negative relation between stocks’ lottery characteristics and expected stock returns (Kumar (2009), Bali, Cakici, and Whitelaw (2011)). Based on Kumar (2009), we constructLOTas the return on a strategy that is long the value-weighted portfolio of stocks with strong lottery characteristics and short the value-weighted portfolio of stocks with weak lottery features. Stocks having strong lottery characteristics are those with above-median IVOL over the past six months, above median idiosyncratic skewness over the past six months, and below-median past month returns. The last set of columns in TableIIIpresent the results of regression analysis including this factor. The estimates of alpha are negative and statistically significant in all cases. The coefficient onLOTis statistically significant only for the Stambaugh and Yuan (2017) model estimated using the 2006 to 2019 sample. These results show that our findings cannot be explained by the stocks’ lottery characteristics.
  We also use an alternative approach, Fama and MacBeth (1973) regressions, to study the ability of SEP sentiment to predict stock returns while controlling for other variables. We conduct the Fama-MacBeth regression analysis using the top market capitalization quintile of CRSP stocks because 82% of the SEPs’ underlying stocks belong to the top market capitalization quintile. The unit of observation is a stock-month. For montht(for example, July 2014), the sample consists of CRSP stocks with share code of 10 or 11 that are in the top CRSP market capitalization quintile on the last trading day of montht– 1 (for example, the last trading day of June 2014). We use the set of monthly samples during the period running from January 2006 through December 2019.
  Each month, we estimate cross-sectional regressions in which we regress the next month's stock return on the current month SEP sentiment measureGt,ntogether with an indicator variable that equals one ifGt,n= 0 and is zero otherwise (I(Gt,n=0))$I({{G_{t,n}} = 0}))$. This dummy variable is included becauseGt,nis nonnegative by construction and some of the stocks for whichGt,n= 0 have negative sentiment. The indicator variable is included to capture the average impact of these stocks’ nonpositive sentiment on returns. The control variables are the log of the market capitalization of the reference stock (FirmSize), the Amihud (2002) illiquidity measure (Illiquidity), short interest normalized by the shares outstanding (ShortInterestRatio), the ratio of the current price to the highest price over the past 52 weeks (52WeekHigh), idiosyncratic volatility (IVOL), the Stambaugh, Yu, and Yuan (2015) mispricing measure (Mispricing), past returns (Return(t)and Return(t− 1)), a dummy variable that equals one if the stock is a lottery stock (Lottery) based on Kumar (2009), an alternative lottery measure defined as the average of the five largest daily returns during montht(MAX5) based on Bali, Cakici, and Whitelaw (2011), and Google trends search volume (SVI). The construction of the Google trends search volume variableSVIis described inAppendix.
  We report the time-series averages of the regression coefficients andt-statistics based on Newey-West standard errors in TableIV. The coefficient on the SEP sentiment measureGt,nis negative and significant in all specifications, regardless of the control variables included. These results are consistent with those from the calendar-time regressions reported in TablesItoIII, and provide additional evidence consistent with the hypothesis that SEP-based sentiment predicts future stock returns at the individual stock level.
  Turning to the control variables, the estimated coefficients on the Stambaugh, Yu, and Yuan (2015) mispricing measure are negative and significant in the four specifications that include this variable, though in one of the four specifications the coefficient is significant at only the 10% level. The coefficients onFirmSizeand the Amihud illiquidity measureIlliquidityare significant in some specifications. The point estimates of the coefficients onShortInterest, 52WeekHigh, andIVOLare negative, as expected, but not significant. The estimated coefficients on the remaining control variables are insignificant.
  In the previous analyses, we used monthly SEP sentiment,Gt,n, to predict returns during the subsequent month (t+ 1). Now we examine the ability ofGt,nto predict returns during monthst+ 2 andt+ 3 using the Fama and French (2015) three-factor model as a benchmark. Specifically, we repeat the regression analysis of SEP sentiment-weighted calendar-time portfolio returns but now use returns during the second (Rt+2=∑n=1NGt,nrt+2,n)${R_{t + 2}} = \sum\nolimits_{n = 1}^N {G_{t,n}}{r_{t + 2,n}})$and third (Rt+3=∑n=1NGt,nrt+3,n)${R_{t + 3}} = \sum\nolimits_{n = 1}^N {G_{t,n}}{r_{t + 3,n}})$months after issuance and regress them on the factor realizations in monthst+ 2 andt+ 3, respectively. We report the results of these regressions in TableV. Using the 2004 to 2019 and 2006 to 2019 samples, for the regressions explainingRt+2the estimates of alpha are −63 bps (t-statistic −2.16) and −80 bps (t-statistic −2.61), respectively. The estimated alphas in the regressions explainingRt+3are smaller and statistically insignificant. Thus, the results provide some evidence that SEP sentiment during monthtpredicts returns in montht+ 2, and no evidence that it predicts returns in montht+ 3.
  We also examine the daily abnormal performance of the SEPs’ underlying stocks during the first two months following the issuance month. For each issuance month, we form a portfolio using the SEP sentiment measureG, as we did for the monthly abnormal return results we report in TableII. We then compute the daily portfolio returns for each trading date during a 121-day window [−60, 60] centered on the last trading date of the issuance month. We use the daily portfolio returns, together with daily factor returns, to estimate the parameters of the Fama-French three-factor model and the associated daily abnormal returns, where for each trade date the abnormal return is the sum of the intercept and the regression residual. For each issuance month, we cumulate the daily abnormal returns starting from the first trade date in the postissuance month (that is, date +1), resulting in a time series of daily CARs. We repeat the process for each issuance month, and finally compute the cross-sectional average CARs, in event time.
  Figure5displays these cross-sectional average CARs for dates in the interval [1, 50], using data from the two samples 2004 to 2019 and 2006 to 2019. For both samples, the CARs decline rapidly for the first 21 days, reaching −1.19% (−1.31%) at date 21 for the 2004 to 2019 (2006 to 2019) sample. These CARs are similar to the monthly abnormal returns of −1.25% (−1.33%) for the Fama-French three-factor model that we report in TableI, Panel A. The CARs decline less rapidly during the next 21 days, reaching −1.74% (−2.12%) for the 2004 to 2019 (2006 to 2019) sample on date 42. The changes in the CARs from date 21 to date 42, −1.74 – (−1.19%) = −0.55% and −2.12% – (−1.31%) = −0.81% for the two samples, are similar to the abnormal returns of −0.63% and −0.80% for the next postissuance month that we report in TableV, Panel A. Figure5also shows that the CARs either stop declining or decline more slowly after about date +40, consistent with the results in TableV, Panel B.
  The results in TablesIIIandIVaddress the possibility that SEP issues explain underlying stock returns during the postissuance month because they are correlated with risk exposures or firm characteristics that explain stock returns. Another possibility is that part of the average abnormal return during the postissuance month is explained by issuer delta hedging of SEP issues. Most SEP issues are priced based on closing stock prices on the pricing (trade) date, providing issuers with an incentive to impact the pricing date closing stock price (Henderson, Pearson, and Wang (2020)). Henderson, Pearson, and Wang (2020) show that, when issuers have such an incentive, issuer delta hedging of SEP issues increases pricing date closing stock prices by about 30 bps. Henderson, Pearson, and Wang (2020) also show that these pricing date price movements are fully reversed by the end of the second subsequent trading day. When SEPs are priced and hedged on one of the last two trading days of a month, these reversals of the positive pricing date abnormal returns will occur during the first postissuance month, and thus could conceivably explain part of the returns during the postissuance month.
  Fewer than 5% of SEP issues are priced on each of the last two trading dates of a month, the price impact and subsequent reversal of about 30 bps is much smaller than the average abnormal return during the first postissuance month of about 100 to 130 bps, and the 30 bps abnormal return and subsequent reversal is found only for the subset of large issues for which the issuer has an incentive to impact closing date closing prices.26In addition, the daily CARs displayed in Figure5show that the abnormal performance is not concentrated in the first few days of the immediate postissuance month. For these reasons, it seems unlikely that issuer delta hedging drives the results regarding the abnormal returns in the month following SEP issues. Nonetheless, we explore this possibility by recomputing the measuresGt,nexcluding SEPs priced during the last two trading days of each month, and then using the modified measures to form portfolios and estimate the abnormal returns using calendar-time regression analyses that are otherwise identical to those in TablesIandII. We report the results of these additional calendar-time regressions inInternet AppendixTablesIAVIIandIAVIII. The results of the calendar-time regressions using the modifiedGt,nmeasures in TablesIAVIIandIAVIIIare very similar to the results in TablesIandII, respectively. In fact, the point estimates of the regression intercepts (abnormal returns) in TablesIAVIIandIAVIIIare generally slightly larger than the corresponding point estimates in TablesIandII. These results indicate that issuer delta-hedging has little or no impact on the main results reported in TablesIandII.
article.IV. Additional Evidence that SEP Issuanc:
We now describe other analyses that provide evidence consistent with the hypothesis that SEP issuance volumes proxy for retail investor sentiment. These other analyses include examining whether an aggregate sentiment measure constructed from SEP issuance volumes is correlated with the Baker-Wurgler sentiment index, exploring whether proxies for investor attention and sentiment explain SEP issues, and examining the returns around the earnings announcement dates shortly after the SEPs’ pricing dates.
  Our sentiment measureGt,ndefined in equation (1) is for individual stocks. For each montht, scaled SEP issuance volumesQt,n/Vt−1,nare normalized so that the sum∑n=1NGt,n$\sum\nolimits_{n = 1}^N {G_{t,n}}$= 1. Thus, there is no reason to expect the individual stock measuresGt,nto be correlated with the Baker-Wurgler sentiment index, which is an aggregate market-wide measure. However, we can construct an aggregate sentiment measure by summing each month's SEP issuance volumes across stocks. If SEP issuances reflect investor sentiment, then one would expect these aggregate issuance volumes to be correlated with the Baker-Wurgler index.
  To test this prediction, for each month we compute the cross-sectional sum of the unnormalized proceedsQt=∑NQt,n${Q_t} =\sum\nolimits^{N} {Q_{t,n}}$. We then construct a three-month moving average MA(Q)t= (Qt+Qt−1+Qt−2)/3 of the aggregate SEP proceeds and examine whether the time series of the moving average is related to the time series of the Baker-Wurgler index. Figure6plots this series along with the Baker-Wurgler sentiment indexSENT, using SEP issuance data from 2004 to 2018.27The figure reveals a clear relation between aggregate SEPs proceeds and the Baker-Wurgler index. The correlations between MA(Q) and the Baker-Wurgler index are high, 0.49 and 0.53, using the SEP data starting in January 2004 or 2006, respectively. The correlations are similar, 0.49 and 0.55, if we use a six-month moving average of aggregate SEP proceeds. That aggregate SEP issuance volumes correlate strongly with the Baker-Wurgler sentiment index validates our claim that SEP issuances reflect investor sentiment.
  We report the results in TableVI. Columns (1) to (3), (4) to (6), and (7) to (9) display the results of models estimated using one-month returns, overlapping three-month returns, and overlapping six-month returns, respectively. The results indicate that MA(Q) is a significant predictor of future three- and six-month market returns, and a marginally significant predictor of one-month returns (t-statistic −1.67). The Baker-Wurgler index significantly predicts six-month returns when it is the only covariate. When we include both MA(Q) andSENTin the specifications, neither covariate is a significant predictor of one-month returns; MA(Q) is a significant predictor of three- and six-month returns, andSENTis not. Thus, during the time period of our sample MA(Q) predicts returns at least as well asSENT.
  We estimate Tobit regression models to study the relation between SEP monthly issuance volumes and a number of covariates, focusing on covariates that proxy for retail investor attention and mispricing. The sample is the same as that used in the Fama and MacBeth (1973) regression analysis, except that the sample for the Tobit regressions ends in 2018 because the data for the Baker-Wurgler sentiment index were only available through 2018 at the time we collected the data.
  We use the issuance volume of SEPs based on stocknscaled by market capitalization, that is,Qt,n/Vt−1,n${Q_{t,n}}/{V_{t - 1,n}}$as the dependent variable rather thanGt,nbecauseGt,nis bounded from above by one and thus does not fit well with the normality assumption in the Tobit model. The variableQt,n/Vt−1,n${Q_{t,n}}/{V_{t - 1,n}}$is always nonnegative and is zero when there is no SEP issue linked to stocknin montht, which occurs frequently. The explanatory variables include: Google trends search volume (SVI), the Stambaugh, Yu, and Yuan (2015) mispricing measure, the Baker-Wurgler sentiment indexSENT, the option-to-stock trading volume (O/S) ratio, the option implied volatility (the average across call options with strike to stock price ratio between 0.9 and 1.1 and 60 days or less to expiration), past stock returns (over the preceding week, the second trailing week, the preceding month, the two-month period formed from the second and third trailing months, and the three-month period formed from the fourth through sixth trailing months), and past stock volatility (over the preceding month, the two-month period formed from the second and third trailing months, and the three-month period formed from the fourth through sixth trailing months). We also control for the firm size as measured by the natural logarithm of the previous month-end market capitalization, and include calendar month fixed effects and industry dummy variables based on the Fama-French 12-industry classification.
  TableVIIpresents the Tobit model results.28All specifications include lagged values of the Baker-Wurgler index, implied volatility (IV), lagged returns, measures of realized volatility, natural log of market capitalization, and fixed effects. The first regression does not include theSVIattention measure, the mispricing measure, and the O/S ratio. The second and third models add theSVIand mispricing measures, respectively. The fourth specification includes both of these variables, and the last specification adds the O/S ratio.
  The results show that retail demand revealed in SEP issuances is positively related to retail investor attention as measured bySVI, as the coefficient on this variable is positive and statistically significant in every specification in which it appears. This is consistent with the finding that retail investors tend to purchase attention-grabbing stocks (Barber and Odean (2008)). The estimate of the coefficient on the mispricing factor is also positive, and highly statistically significant: across the specifications that include this variable, the smallestt-statistic is 24.51. This result is consistent with the hypothesis that more overpriced stocks are associated with greater SEP sentiment. The coefficient on the Baker-Wurgler index is highly significant in every model, consistent with SEP issuances being related to sentiment.
  Retail SEPs demand is also positively related to the O/S ratio in the results in column (5), though the interpretation of this coefficient is unclear because the determinants of options trading volume are not well understood. Regardless, the significantly positive coefficient on the O/S ratio suggests that the factors that cause investors to trade options are correlated with the factors that lead to demand for SEPs.
  The coefficient on implied volatility is positive and highly significant in all specifications. There is a natural supply side interpretation of this coefficient. Célérier and Vallée (2017) argue that issuers structure SEPs to cater to yield-chasing investors who focus on the SEPs’ headline rates. Since the investor sells and the issuer retains the embedded options, it is easier for the issuer to structure a SEP with a high headline rate if the underlying stock volatility is high, because then the embedded options are more valuable.
  The significantly negative coefficient estimates before recent stock returns over the preceding one week, the preceding month, and the trailing three months show that short-term loser stocks are more likely to attract SEPs purchasers, possibly because these investors are short-term contrarian buyers who believe these stock temporarily underperform and are overconfident about the future. However, the economic magnitudes of the coefficient estimates are small. Finally, SEPs issuances are also positively correlated with past volatility over the trailing one through six months. A demand side interpretation of this result is that SEP investors prefer SEPs on high-volatility stocks, perhaps because volatility is correlated with investor attention. These results are consistent with the hypothesis that SEPs issuances reflect retail investor sentiment.
  If SEPs reference stocks are overvalued because investors are overconfident about firms’ prospects, the overvaluation should be corrected when outcomes are realized (Bernard and Thomas (1990)). Because relevant information is often released on earnings announcement dates, any over(under)valuation due to biased expectations about future cash flows should be partially corrected by the subsequent earnings announcements, resulting in negative (positive) returns over a short window around the announcements (Lewellen (2010)). Since we hypothesize that the SEPs’ underlying stocks are overvalued, we expect to observe such corrections during short windows around earnings announcements following the SEPs issuances. We investigate this prediction by examining the reference stock returns around earnings announcements within 60 days of the SEP issuances.
  In TableVIII, we report the average market-adjusted stock returns during short windows around the first earnings announcements within 15, 30, and 60 days following the SEPs issuances. The results in the table show that the average market-adjusted returns over three- and four-day windows around earnings announcements within 15 days of SEPs issuances are negative, −36 and −32 bps, respectively, witht-statistics of −2.61 and −2.28. Thus, the returns around earnings announcements shortly after SEP issuances are a significant fraction of the abnormal return during the first month following the issuance month. This is consistent with the hypothesis that SEP issues reflect overvaluation, with some of the overvaluation being corrected when news is released.29The average returns around the more distant announcements within 30 and 60 days after SEPs issuances are also negative, with magnitudes andt-statistics that decline as the horizon lengthens.
  We also expect the underlying stock returns over small windows around the earnings announcement dates to be correlated with the sentiment measureG. We explore this by starting with the sample of top market capitalization quintile stocks used in the Fama-MacBeth and Tobit regressions, and then in each monthtrestricting attention to the stocks that have an earnings announcement during montht. Letting the earnings announcement date be date 0, we regress stock returns over the windows [0, 2] and [−1, 2] on the sentiment measureGt−1,ncomputed using SEPs issuances during montht− 1, an indicator variableI(Gt−1,n= 0) that equals one ifGt−1,n= 0, and the control variables used in the Aboody et al. (2018) cross-sectional regressions that explain earnings announcement returns. The first control variable is earnings before extraordinary items (Earnings), as reported in the IBES database and standardized by the per-share stock price at the close of trading two days prior to that announcement. The second control variable (AnalystForecast) is the consensus analyst earnings forecast as of two days prior to the announcement, standardized by the per-share stock price at the close of trading two days prior to the earnings announcement.
  TableIXreports the cross-sectional results. Scanning across the columns reveals that the sentiment measureGis negatively correlated with the returns over both windows, consistent with the stocks’ overpricing partially correcting around earnings announcements. The results also show thatEarningsandAnalystForecastare positively and negatively correlated with the returns, which is consistent with the literature showing that the stock prices react to earnings and earnings surprises.30
article.V. Conclusion:
This paper proposes a new cross-sectional measure of investor sentiment based on issuances of retail SEPs. The SEP sentiment measure strongly predicts negative abnormal stock returns. Using the 2004 to 2019 sample period and the Fama and French (1993, 2015)) and Carhart (1997) models, the abnormal returns of calendar-time portfolios constructed using the sentiment measure are between −1.09% and −1.25% per month, with highly significantt-statistics between −3.83 and −4.51, respectively. We obtain similar results using the Hou, Xue, and Zhang (2015) and Hou et al. (2021)q-factor models. Using the Stambaugh and Yuan (2017) and Daniel, Hirshleifer, and Sun (2020) mispricing and behavioral factor models, the portfolio alphas remain large and statistically significant, though the magnitudes of the abnormal returns are smaller than those obtained using the other models. The results using the Stambaugh and Yuan (2017) and Daniel, Hirshleifer, and Sun (2020) models as benchmarks indicate that the SEP sentiment measure provides incremental information about returns that is not captured by the mispricing and behavioral factors.
  We add additional factors based on IVOL, short interest, the 52-week high, short-term reversal, and lottery characteristics to the factor models. The estimated alphas remain large and statistically significant when the additional factors are included. Consistent with the results of the calendar-time regressions, we also find that the sentiment measure predicts returns in Fama and MacBeth (1973) regressions that control for a number of other variables known to predict returns.
  We interpret the negative abnormal returns as reversals of overvaluation due to investor sentiment. Henderson and Pearson (2011) and Vokatá (2021) find that SEPs’ markups are so large that under reasonable assumptions the expected returns of SEPs are less than the risk-free return, and Vokatá (2021) shows that the payoffs of a large fraction of U.S. YEPs are dominated by those of simple combinations of bonds and listed options. This makes it unlikely that many sophisticated investors purchase SEPs, consistent with the hypothesis that SEPs issuance volumes proxy for the demands of unsophisticated noise traders.
  Aggregate SEP issuance volumes during each month are highly correlated with the Baker-Wurgler sentiment index, validating our claim that SEP issuances reflect investor sentiment. Tobit regression analysis shows that proxies for investor attention, misevaluation, and sentiment help explain SEP issuance volumes. We find significantly negative market-adjusted returns during short windows around the first earnings announcement dates following SEPs issuances. This finding is consistent with some of the overvaluation of the SEPs’ underlying stocks correcting with the release of earnings news.
  Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
article.Appendix: Construction of the Google Tre:
This appendix describes the construction of the GoogleSVImeasure that we use in the results we report in TablesIVandVII. Google Trends provides the number of searches at a monthly frequency from 2004 to the present. Da, Engelberg, and Gao (2011) is the pioneering study on the relation between GoogleSVIand investor attention, establishing thatSVIis a proxy for retail investor attention. We mainly follow their steps to construct theSVIfor our sample of CRSP top-quintile market capitalization common stocks (those with security code 10 or 11), deviating only where necessary to construct a cross-sectional measure that is comparable across stocks.
  Following Da, Engelberg, and Gao (2011), we use stock ticker symbols rather than company names in the queries due to variation in company names and to avoid gathering information on searches due to unrelated nonfinancial purposes. There are some exceptions. Ticker symbols that are combinations of letters that are frequently used in nonfinancial domains, for example, “ACT,” “ALL,” “AN,” “CAR,” “GAS,” “N,” and “SEE,” typically have very largeSVIs. We manually identify these tickers, and for them use the corresponding company name in place of the ticker symbol. This is necessary for 5.1% of the 2,195 sample stocks.
  To make theSVIs comparable across different stocks, we include the same ticker, AACC, in each five-stock query, and also execute a one-stock query for AACC. We then use the results for the ticker AACC to rescale all of the results to make them comparable across queries and stocks, as follows.
  By doing this, all the search volumes are presented in the same “unit,” that is, scaled by the time-series maximum number of searches for AACC, which in our sample occurs in August 2011.
  We fill in missing values using interpolation, or when interpolation is not possible, extrapolation. Finally, we use the rescaledSVIs for 2,092 and 1,974 tickers during the period running from 2004 through 2019 in TablesIVandVII, respectively.
